one_liner: A complexity index that scores political promises.
search_doc:
A complexity index that scores political promises.
Mechanisms: data-as-content, legislative-advocacy, accountability-tool, election-cycle-hijack
Idea: For the first time, we decided to analyze the candidates' proposals through data analysis. We developed an indicator capable of measuring the implementation difficulty of each candidate's proposals: 'The Complexity Index'.
Insight: Political promises about disability rights stay safely abstract — but turning each promise into a measurable 'complexity score' forces candidates to defend the gap between rhetoric and feasibility.

full_record.file_path: 2024/SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS/BRONZE/Absurd Promises - ASDRA (VML, Buenos Aires).jpg

extracted.tagline: We analyzed the most unreal proposal to create a new law. How hard to fulfill are the candidates' proposals?
extracted.context: In Argentina, people with Down syndrome receive an annual disability certificate due to an absurd law ignored by politicians.
extracted.idea: For the first time, we decided to analyze the candidates' proposals through data analysis. We developed an indicator capable of measuring the implementation difficulty of each candidate's proposals: 'The Complexity Index'.
execution: Worked alongside social scientists, economists, political scientists, lawyers and journalists
Analysed hundreds of proposals from the 2023 presidential election
Built the 'Complexity Index' with four axes: Money, Time, Steps, Political Will
Ranked candidates by how unreal their proposals were
Released the index during the campaign so voters could compare promises with the reality of disability law
metrics: 645% (figure on board next to a candidate's portrait)
1953% (figure on board next to a candidate's portrait)

inferred.insight: Political promises about disability rights stay safely abstract — but turning each promise into a measurable 'complexity score' forces candidates to defend the gap between rhetoric and feasibility.
inferred.one_liner: A complexity index that scores political promises.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Quantified Argentine political candidates' disability-related promises on a feasibility index, contributing to the approval of a new disability law that benefits five million people.

visual.style_description: Dark high-contrast political-graphic layout with two large grainy black-and-white candidate close-ups framed by red rectangles, neon green percentage figures floating over each portrait; a parliamentary-chamber arc graphic at the bottom centre; campaign and complexity-index callouts on either side.

idea.rationale: Building a 'Complexity Index' that scores presidential candidates' promises against four feasibility axes (Money, Time, Steps, Political Will) turns intangible political rhetoric into a comparable, journalist-friendly metric — a clean accountability-tool idea borrowed from finance and applied to civic life. The hook is more analytic than emotional, which limits memorability but suits the legislative-shift ambition.

strategy.rationale: ASDRA is the Argentine Down Syndrome Association, an advocacy NGO whose remit is exactly to defend the rights of people with Down syndrome — so building a tool that scrutinises politicians' commitments to disability law is direct mission-fit and squarely advances SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities) and SDG 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions). The context names an absurd annual-disability-certificate law as the lived grievance, which is a credible Argentine policy fact, though the board doesn't cite specific data sources for the index methodology. The strategic timing — releasing during the 2023 presidential campaign — is the load-bearing choice that gives the tool teeth.

execution.rationale: The orchestration assembles a credibly multidisciplinary panel — social scientists, economists, political scientists, lawyers and journalists — to score hundreds of proposals, then publishes candidate-by-candidate rankings during the campaign window. Amplification on the board is thin (only mdz is named) and the index leans on the editorial credibility of its expert panel rather than a paid-media push, which is consistent with an NGO budget but limits the visible earned-media trail.

impact.rationale: The headline business outcome for an advocacy NGO is legislative change, and the board claims a 'new disability law approved' which would be the right unit of proof if directly attributable — though the campaign is one input among many in a national legislative process and the board does not isolate Absurd Promises' specific causal contribution. '+5 million people impacted' is an audience claim rather than an audited outcome, and the two large percentage figures (645% and 1953%) appear next to candidate portraits without a clear base or unit, making them the campaign's main RIGOUR pattern-1 violation. '100% benefited' is an unanchored superlative.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the Complexity Index is a smart accountability mechanism credibly tied to a new Argentine disability law, but the supporting figures — 645%, 1953%, '5 million impacted', '100% benefited' — are largely unanchored against a base.

one_liner: No car, no job. No job, no loan. No loan, no car.
search_doc:
No car, no job. No job, no loan. No loan, no car.
Mechanisms: branded-utility-for-sdg, purpose-driven-product, partnership-with-public-institution, mobility-as-employment-access
Idea: Renault Group provides cars for free to people during their job-trial periods, so that they only start to pay at affordable conditions once their job is secured. Cars made available through 50 dealerships in mobility deserts and on FranceTravail.fr (the French public employment service).
Insight: Without a car you can't get a job; without a job you can't get a loan; without a loan you can't get a car — mobility is the hidden gate to employment for France's poorest.

full_record.file_path: 2024/SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS/GRAND PRIX/Cars To Work - Renault (Publicis Conseil, Paris).jpeg

extracted.tagline: No car, no job. No job, no loan. No loan, no car.
extracted.context: A vicious circle for society's most fragile, a creator of social exclusion: 40% of French people live in areas with no public transportation, places where a car is the only solution. 54% of French job-seekers have already turned down a position because they have no mobility solution.
extracted.idea: Renault Group provides cars for free to people during their job-trial periods, so that they only start to pay at affordable conditions once their job is secured. Cars made available through 50 dealerships in mobility deserts and on FranceTravail.fr (the French public employment service).
execution: 50 Cars to Work dealerships in French mobility deserts
6,000 Cars to Work vehicles made available
Partnership with France Travail public employment service
Three-month free trial period before affordable payment terms

inferred.insight: Without a car you can't get a job; without a job you can't get a loan; without a loan you can't get a car — mobility is the hidden gate to employment for France's poorest.
inferred.one_liner: No car, no job. No job, no loan. No loan, no car.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Renault reframed car ownership as an anti-exclusion lever, deploying 6,000 vehicles through 50 dealerships in French mobility deserts and reaching 8.8 million people with a free-to-trial scheme tied to the public employment service.

visual.style_description: Wide cinematic landscape photo of a small white Renault on an empty rural road, with a curved hand-lettered headline 'no car, no job, no loan' wrapping around the car; lower band carries the campaign title, three large metric blocks, four execution thumbnails, a France map, and a customer testimonial quote.

idea.rationale: Reframing the car itself as the entry-point to employment — free during the job-trial, payable only once the job is secured — turns Renault's core product into an anti-exclusion mechanism rather than a campaign about exclusion. The 'no car, no job, no loan' loop is a memorable, near-aphoristic articulation of a structural poverty trap that the work then literally breaks.

strategy.rationale: Renault Group is the French legacy automaker whose mass-market mobility heritage makes 'mobility-as-employment-access' a genuine mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose, and the work explicitly advances SDG targets around decent work and reduced inequalities. The board anchors the insight with hard, verifiable French statistics — 40% of French citizens live in areas without public transport and 54% of job-seekers have already declined a position for lack of mobility — and the France Travail public-employment partnership confers state-level credibility on the framing.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is infrastructure, not communication: 6,000 vehicles physically deployed through 50 dedicated dealerships sited in France's mobility deserts, with the offer surfaced directly inside FranceTravail.fr where job-seekers already search. Press amplification spans national TV (Canal+, M6, TMC, W9), radio (RMC) and quality print (Le Monde, Le Figaro, Arte), turning a service rollout into a national-policy conversation.

impact.rationale: The headline outcomes are structural and absolute rather than percentage-based: 6,000 cars actually placed and 50 dealerships actually built — the right business proof for a mobility-as-utility intervention, since the unit of impact is a vehicle in a household's driveway. The 8.8 million-French-people reach figure is an entrant-reported audience claim rather than an audited outcome, but the partnership with France Travail and the breadth of unpaid national-press coverage are credible signals of cultural footprint. Cohort-level outcome data (how many of the 6,000 drivers were hired and retained) is not disclosed on the board, which is the only soft spot in an otherwise unusually material impact case.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because Renault rebuilt a piece of its core business — 6,000 cars and 50 dealerships sited in French mobility deserts and plumbed into the public employment service — into a working anti-exclusion utility, not a campaign about one.

one_liner: AI reroutes planes to stop making warming clouds.
search_doc:
AI reroutes planes to stop making warming clouds.
Mechanisms: ai-powered-routing, data-as-content, branded-utility-for-sdg, partnership-with-industry
Idea: Google Research teamed up with American Airlines and Breakthrough Energy to combine satellite imagery, weather and flight path data, using AI to build forecast maps that let pilots choose routes that avoid creating contrails.
Insight: Half of aviation's climate impact isn't the CO2 - it's the clouds planes leave behind, and those clouds are avoidable if pilots simply know which routes to skip.

full_record.file_path: 2024/SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS/SILVER/Contrails - Google (Google, Mountain View _ Google Creative Lab, New York).jpg

extracted.tagline: Contrails, short for condensation trails, are the line-shaped clouds that you sometimes see behind airplanes. They have a surprisingly large impact on our climate, but they could be avoidable.
extracted.context: Clouds created by contrails account for roughly 35% of aviation's global warming impact, over half the impact of the world's jet fuel, according to the 2022 IPCC report. Though planes can't fly without burning fuel, they can fly without creating contrails - the challenge is knowing which routes will create them.
extracted.idea: Google Research teamed up with American Airlines and Breakthrough Energy to combine satellite imagery, weather and flight path data, using AI to build forecast maps that let pilots choose routes that avoid creating contrails.
execution: AI model trained on satellite imagery, weather and flight path data
Forecast maps delivered to pilots to guide route choices
70 American Airlines test flights flown over six months using the AI predictions
Post-flight satellite analysis to verify contrail reduction
metrics: 54% reduction in contrails when pilots used the AI predictions
Additional fuel burn ~0.3% if scaled across an airline's fleet
Contrail warming reduction is 20x greater than the additional CO2 from fuel burned
Aviation accounts for ~2.5% of global CO2 emissions but ~3.5% of global warming
Estimated contrail avoidance cost of $5-25/ton CO2e

inferred.insight: Half of aviation's climate impact isn't the CO2 - it's the clouds planes leave behind, and those clouds are avoidable if pilots simply know which routes to skip.
inferred.one_liner: AI reroutes planes to stop making warming clouds.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A Google/American Airlines/Breakthrough Energy test flight programme delivered a 54% reduction in contrails using AI-routed flights, positioning contrail avoidance as one of the most cost-effective climate solutions for aviation.

visual.style_description: Clean blue editorial board with the word CONTRAILS as huge white display type stretched across the top, a single jet leaving a real contrail trace through the layout; three text columns (Challenge, Idea, Results) sit below an italic tagline, with a small earth-with-contrails photo at left and a hero 54% reduction stat panel at right.

idea.rationale: Surfacing the counter-intuitive insight that half of aviation's warming impact is contrails rather than CO2, and then turning route choice into the lever, is a strong piece of data storytelling. The idea sits closer to a science communication and engineering pilot than a campaignable creative concept, which keeps it on-brief for SDG 13 but slightly limits its iconic memorability.

strategy.rationale: Google is a global technology company whose climate strategy has long centred on applying AI and search-scale data to environmental problems, with explicit SDG 13 (Climate Action) commitments. The partnership with American Airlines and Bill Gates's Breakthrough Energy gives the work institutional authority that matches Google's role as a research and infrastructure player rather than a behaviour-change marketer. The 35% / 2.5% / 3.5% statistics carry a specific 2022 IPCC source on the board, which is the strongest possible kind of insight backing.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is a real flight programme rather than a communications push: AI forecast maps built on satellite, weather and flight-path data, then validated across 70 American Airlines test flights over six months, with post-flight satellite analysis to verify. Distribution is essentially via a peer-reviewed research artefact and industry partnership rather than mass media, which is correct for the audience of pilots, airline operators and aviation regulators. The board carries no consumer press logos because the target audience is not consumers.

impact.rationale: The headline '54% reduction in contrails' is anchored to a defined test cohort (70 flights, six months), which is the right business unit for a climate pilot. The supporting framing — '20x more warming reduction than the additional CO2 burned' and '$5-25/ton CO2e avoidance cost' — is credibly modelled but remains a research projection rather than a fleet-scale operational result; no absolute tonnes-of-CO2e avoided are claimed. The contrail problem isn't yet solved at airline scale, so the impact narrative is a proof-of-concept rather than a delivered SDG outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the science is rigorous and the 54% reduction is anchored, but the result is a 70-flight pilot rather than fleet-scale CO2e abatement — a credible proof-of-concept for SDG 13 rather than a delivered outcome.

one_liner: The rice canister becomes a secret bank account.
search_doc:
The rice canister becomes a secret bank account.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, branded-utility-for-sdg, community-as-co-author, purpose-driven-product
Idea: ESAF Small Finance Bank, a social bank catering to marginalised households, had a simple 'inside-the-box' solution: present the Dabba Savings Account - the secret savings account in the kitchen. They created the Dabba with a partition function in plain sight. While the top section can be used to store rice as usual, the hidden bottom section could hide cash.
Insight: Rural Indian women already hide cash inside the household rice canister - giving that canister a hidden bank account turns an existing habit into a regulated savings product.

full_record.file_path: 2024/SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS/BRONZE/Dabba Savings Account - Esaf Small Finance Bank (McCann Worldgroup India).jpeg

extracted.tagline: The secret savings account in the kitchen.
extracted.context: Financial inclusion remains a big challenge for India. Almost 25% of rural Indian women are still without bank accounts. Lack of education, complex paperwork and less accessibility are key barriers. Women typically 'save' their cash inside a rice canister popularly known as a Dabba - they believe it brings prosperity and growth, but this way their money doesn't grow. It is also taken away by men in their family.
extracted.idea: ESAF Small Finance Bank, a social bank catering to marginalised households, had a simple 'inside-the-box' solution: present the Dabba Savings Account - the secret savings account in the kitchen. They created the Dabba with a partition function in plain sight. While the top section can be used to store rice as usual, the hidden bottom section could hide cash.
execution: Co-created Dabba rice canisters with a hidden partition that stores cash inside the kitchen
Distributed Dabbas at the women's monthly community gatherings, opening their new ESAF Savings Account
Could continue their habit of saving safely while building micro-ATM-enabled, biometric (Aadhaar) bank accounts
Equipped the local shopkeepers with micro-ATMs using India's unique biometric system
metrics: 8 rural districts of the Southern Indian state - Tamil Nadu reached

inferred.insight: Rural Indian women already hide cash inside the household rice canister - giving that canister a hidden bank account turns an existing habit into a regulated savings product.
inferred.one_liner: The rice canister becomes a secret bank account.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a household rice canister into a hidden, biometric-linked savings account that opened 121,670 accounts for unbanked rural women in Tamil Nadu with zero paid media.

visual.style_description: Dark green editorial board with a central portrait of an Indian woman in a sari proudly holding up the modified silver rice canister; campaign title sits at the top in white serif type; left column carries Problem/Idea blocks with the ESAF and 'Dabba Savings Account' lockups, right side a black Execution/Results panel with four large zero-spend stats above a 'How It Works' photo strip.

idea.rationale: Turning the household rice canister — the kitchen object women already use to hide cash from their husbands — into a hidden compartment linked to a biometric bank account is a precise everyday-object transformation. It treats the cultural habit as the product spec rather than trying to displace it, which is the rare on-brief idea for SDG 5 / SDG 10 financial inclusion.

strategy.rationale: ESAF Small Finance Bank is an Indian small-finance bank explicitly chartered to serve marginalised rural households, so the work sits inside its statutory mission rather than borrowing purpose; the SDGs in play are SDG 1 (No Poverty), SDG 5 (Gender Equality) and SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities). The insight is grounded in a hard Indian financial-inclusion statistic — almost 25% of rural Indian women remain unbanked — and a culturally specific observation about household cash being appropriated by male relatives, both of which are verifiable. Mission-fit is exact.

execution.rationale: The Dabba canister was co-designed with users and distributed at monthly women's community gatherings, with the savings account linked to micro-ATMs at local shopkeepers using India's Aadhaar biometric rails — the right channel architecture for women who cannot easily travel to a bank branch. The campaign ran with zero paid advertising, PR or earned media spend, so distribution rests entirely on community-network mechanics, which is consistent with the audience but limits scale beyond the eight pilot districts.

impact.rationale: The headline outcome — 121,670 secret accounts opened in eight rural Tamil Nadu districts — is an anchored absolute and the right business metric for a bank acquisition brief, with zero paid spend as a credible efficiency frame. The board does not disclose deposit volume, average balance or the share of accounts still active after onboarding, so the financial-inclusion outcome stops at account opening rather than reaching documented savings behaviour. Geographic scope is explicitly stated (eight districts of Tamil Nadu) rather than overclaimed at national scale.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the mechanic is genuinely insight-true and the 121,670 accounts are anchored to a defined region, but the financial-inclusion case stops at account opening rather than proving sustained savings behaviour.

one_liner: The chip factory becomes a water factory.
search_doc:
The chip factory becomes a water factory.
Mechanisms: branded-utility-for-sdg, byproduct-as-resource, factory-process-reuse, infrastructure-delivered, purpose-driven-product
Idea: Lay's has found a way to create water from the very core of our process — the potato. The potato contains 80% water which is wasted during chip production. We've engineered a method to capture every single drop of it. We rely on an effective and easily installable mechanism that converts the steam released while cooking chips into 120 million litres of water annually. This technology can create water from potatoes in any food-processing industry and ease the pressure on our limited water resources.
Insight: Potatoes are 80% water and chip-making boils that water away as steam — the food industry is silently evaporating a usable freshwater resource.

full_record.file_path: 2024/SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS/BRONZE/Drops of Joy - Lay_s (Leo Burnett, Mumbai).webp

extracted.tagline: Scalable technology that can create water from the potatoes and save our dying water resources.
extracted.context: 10 billion tons of water is used every day worldwide. Consequently our water resources, such as rivers, lakes, and underground water, are depleting due to the immense pressure of this water demand. And as a result, our planet is heading towards an inevitable water crisis.
extracted.idea: Lay's has found a way to create water from the very core of our process — the potato. The potato contains 80% water which is wasted during chip production. We've engineered a method to capture every single drop of it. We rely on an effective and easily installable mechanism that converts the steam released while cooking chips into 120 million litres of water annually. This technology can create water from potatoes in any food-processing industry and ease the pressure on our limited water resources.
execution: On-site condensation rig installed in chip factories
5-step process: vapour collection, condensation, 2-step purification, aeration, natural water
[…] vapour collection from cooking potatoes
Filtered through treatment systems
metrics: 120 million litres of water created per year
7.7 trillion litres of water creatable if implemented industry-wide in the next 50 years
Water enough to fulfill drinking-water requirement of 3,800 people per year
Can fill 75 Olympic-size swimming pools every year

inferred.insight: Potatoes are 80% water and chip-making boils that water away as steam — the food industry is silently evaporating a usable freshwater resource.
inferred.one_liner: The chip factory becomes a water factory.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Lay's engineered a steam-capture system that recovers the water boiled out of potatoes during chip production, yielding 120 million litres a year (enough to fill 75 Olympic pools) and a 7.7 trillion-litre scale-up potential if adopted industry-wide.

visual.style_description: Centre hero photo of a single potato resting on a still water surface with ripples beneath it, headline running down the left; right column shows factory infrastructure thumbnails and a blue-toned data-visualisation grid; bottom strip carries the five-step process diagram with circular icons.

idea.rationale: Capturing the water boiled out of potatoes during chip production and turning a snack factory into a freshwater factory is a cleanly inventive byproduct-as-resource move that lives in the manufacturing process rather than in communications. The '80% of a potato is water' fact is the kind of insight that reframes a familiar product, even if the idea sits closer to engineering case study than campaign in its presentation.

strategy.rationale: Lay's is PepsiCo's global potato-chip brand whose central raw material is the potato, which makes 'water from potatoes' an unusually direct mission-fit rather than a borrowed-purpose move on SDG 6 (Clean Water). The context cites '10 billion tons of water used every day worldwide' as the framing data point, which is a global-scale claim without a sourced reference and reads as a gestural backdrop rather than an Indian-market-specific insight — a soft spot given that the manufacturer is being represented from Mumbai. Brand authority to speak on water is plausible because Lay's owns the upstream water-cost of the supply chain, but the board does not explicitly name the SDG.

execution.rationale: Execution is the five-step on-site condensation rig — vapour collection, condensation, two-step purification, aeration, output as natural water — installed into existing chip-factory infrastructure. Amplification is essentially absent on the board (no press_coverage listed and no media phases described), so the work reads as an engineering rollout documented as a campaign rather than a campaign with a press loop, which limits the visible orchestration.

impact.rationale: 120 million litres of water created per year is an absolute, factory-attributable outcome — the right unit of proof for a manufacturing-process intervention — and the '75 Olympic-size swimming pools' framing makes the scale legible. The headline scale-up potential of 7.7 trillion litres over 50 years if adopted industry-wide is a projection, not a realised outcome, and 'drinking water for 3,800 people per year' from 120M litres implies a very generous per-person allocation that the board does not unpack. Crucially, no press coverage, no documented partner adoption, and no third-party validation are presented, so the impact case rests entirely on entrant-reported engineering metrics.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the chip-factory-as-water-factory mechanism is genuinely on-mission engineering, but the impact case rests on a single factory metric and a 50-year industry-wide projection with no documented third-party adoption.

one_liner: The bottle cap becomes the water treatment plant.
search_doc:
The bottle cap becomes the water treatment plant.
Mechanisms: branded-utility-for-sdg, everyday-object-transformation, frugal-innovation, purpose-driven-product
Idea: Filsa Water, a scientific company committed to developing infrastructure and technology, partnered with Baylor International and the Colombian Red Cross to provide an immediate solution: Filter Caps, a universal bottle cap turned into a powerful water filter by linking two bottles together, allowing water to be purified as it flows through the cap.
Insight: In remote, drought-stricken communities, the limiting factor is not bottled water supply but the absence of a portable, affordable purification tool that fits the bottles people already have.

full_record.file_path: 2024/SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS/GOLD/Filter Caps - Filsa Colombia (Ogilvy Colombia Bogotà).jpg

extracted.tagline: A water treatment plant in a simple cap.
extracted.context: By 2025, half of the world's population will live in water-stressed areas. Around 2.2 billion people lack access to clean and safe drinking water, a serious situation caused by a devastating drought in 2024. Many of these people live in remote areas, where they extract water from unsafe sources, leading to thousands of deaths annually.
extracted.idea: Filsa Water, a scientific company committed to developing infrastructure and technology, partnered with Baylor International and the Colombian Red Cross to provide an immediate solution: Filter Caps, a universal bottle cap turned into a powerful water filter by linking two bottles together, allowing water to be purified as it flows through the cap.
execution: Universal-fit cap that screws onto any standard PET bottle
Filter water by linking two bottles together so water purifies as it flows through
Compact device (3x4 cm), lightweight (24 g)
Made of metals, minerals and natural substances acting as a portable treatment plant
Eliminates microorganisms responsible for waterborne illnesses
100% compostable design
Deployment in Colombia, Argentina and Brazil
metrics: 300K liters of water ready to drink
Cuts the cost by half vs diarrhea medicine
Provides 720 liters of water per day

inferred.insight: In remote, drought-stricken communities, the limiting factor is not bottled water supply but the absence of a portable, affordable purification tool that fits the bottles people already have.
inferred.one_liner: The bottle cap becomes the water treatment plant.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a universal bottle cap into a low-cost portable water filter delivering 1.8M litres of safe water per year to 10K people across Colombia, Argentina and Brazil, at half the cost of diarrhea medicine.

visual.style_description: Cinematic case-board hero of a Colombian girl in a red headscarf using two stacked bottles connected by the Filter Cap, central product packshot of the cap-and-bottle on a dark studio background; right column carries large yellow metrics; bottom strip shows community-use photography and a row of press logos.

idea.rationale: Compressing an entire water treatment plant into a 24 g universal bottle cap that screws onto any standard PET bottle is a category-defining piece of frugal product engineering. The reframe — purify-as-it-pours rather than carry a separate filtration device — is both memorable and natively useful in remote drought-stricken contexts.

strategy.rationale: Filsa Water is a Colombian scientific company positioned as a developer of water infrastructure and technology, partnered here with Baylor International and the Colombian Red Cross to address SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation). The idea is exact mission-fit — a clean-water company building a clean-water product — and the context is anchored by specific data points (2.2 billion people without safe water, the 2024 Colombian drought). The insight that bottles are abundant in remote areas but purification tools aren't is plausible but stated as self-evident rather than quantified on the board.

execution.rationale: The product itself does the campaign work: a compostable, two-bottle screw-link system that requires no power, no infrastructure and no instructions beyond linking. Distribution went through Red Cross networks across Colombia, Argentina and Brazil, with earned press across CBS, MSN, The Drum and El Tiempo amplifying the launch. The choice of NGO partners as the delivery channel is correctly matched to the target population (remote, unbanked, unreachable by retail).

impact.rationale: The board hits the right business outcome for an SDG 6 brief — population served and litres delivered — with anchored absolutes (10K people with safe water, 1.8M litres filtered per year, 720 litres per device per day). The performance claims (70% impurity reduction, 90% heavy metal elimination) are stated without an external lab citation, so they read as entrant-reported rather than independently verified. The 'cuts cost by half vs diarrhea medicine' line is a credible cost-of-prevention frame but the comparator base isn't disclosed.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because a clean-water brand turned its own product packaging into the SDG 6 utility — a portable treatment plant delivered through Red Cross channels with anchored litres-and-people numbers, not just impressions.

one_liner: The dam becomes a doorway for migrating fish.
search_doc:
The dam becomes a doorway for migrating fish.
Mechanisms: branded-utility-for-sdg, infrastructure-delivered, ai-species-recognition, biodiversity-as-product, partnership-with-ngo
Idea: Engineering a smart hydraulic underwater bridge that recognises fish species and enables migratory fish to safely travel back river streams.
Insight: A hydropower dam is a wall for migratory fish — but it can become a doorway if the dam learns to recognise who is trying to swim through.

full_record.file_path: 2024/SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS/SILVER/FishHeart - Fortum (TBWA Screen, Helsinki _ Fishheart, Helsinki.jpg

extracted.tagline: Attracting new business by becoming the most biodiversity-friendly energy company in Finland.
extracted.context: Hydro power dams prevent thousands of fish from returning home every year.
extracted.idea: Engineering a smart hydraulic underwater bridge that recognises fish species and enables migratory fish to safely travel back river streams.
execution: Smart hydraulic underwater bridge for migratory fish
AI fish-recognition system identifies species
Deployed at hydropower dam sites in Finland
Safe entrance / safe floorspace for non-migratory species
Exit to upstream channel

inferred.insight: A hydropower dam is a wall for migratory fish — but it can become a doorway if the dam learns to recognise who is trying to swim through.
inferred.one_liner: The dam becomes a doorway for migrating fish.
inferred.qualitative_summary: An AI-driven hydraulic 'fish heart' bridge let 13,300 migratory fish across 22 species cross Finnish hydropower dams safely, with the system now adopted in 20 countries by 2024 — positioning Fortum as Finland's most biodiversity-friendly energy company.

visual.style_description: Hero CGI photo of an Oulujoki lake trout suspended underwater behind a deep-blue gradient backdrop, flanked by left/right columns of Challenge/Idea/Results text and AI-fish badge; bottom strip shows four real-world product shots of the FishHeart bridge installation at a dam.

idea.rationale: An AI-equipped hydraulic underwater bridge that recognises fish species and ferries migratory fish across hydropower dams turns the dam from a wall into a doorway — a literal, engineered solution to one of the cleanest paradoxes in renewable energy (clean power blocking biodiversity). The species-recognition layer is what makes it feel inventive rather than just another fish ladder.

strategy.rationale: Fortum is the Finnish state-majority-owned energy utility whose hydropower fleet directly causes the problem the FishHeart solves, so addressing migratory-fish blockage is on-mission rather than borrowed — and the work advances SDG 14 (Life Below Water) and SDG 15 (Life on Land) at the dam interface. The strategic positioning to 'become the most biodiversity-friendly energy company in Finland' frames CSR as a competitive differentiator in a market where corporate buyers increasingly screen on biodiversity metrics. The context paragraph is thin on data points — 'thousands of fish' is gestural rather than sourced — which limits insight-veracity rigour.

execution.rationale: Execution is in the hardware itself: the smart hydraulic bridge with species-recognition AI, safe entry chamber, safe floorspace for non-migratory species and upstream exit, deployed at actual Finnish hydropower dam sites. Amplification is narrow — BBC StoryWorks and World Energy Council are the named platforms — leaning on industry-credibility channels rather than mass-media saturation, which is consistent with a B2B-and-policy positioning.

impact.rationale: The headline outcomes are absolute, not percentage-based: 13,300 fish actually transferred and 22 species preserved, which are the right unit of proof for a biodiversity-infrastructure intervention. The '100% fish safety' claim is unanchored — 100% of which cohort, measured how, over what window — and reads as a self-reported KPI rather than an audited outcome. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the AI-driven dam doorway is genuinely inventive infrastructure and on-mission for Fortum, but the impact case rests on a 100% safety claim without a stated cohort and a 'thousands of fish' context that isn't sourced.

'700+ companies use Inployable' and '24 countries' are credible adoption-scale signals, and the LinkedIn skills-taxonomy change is a durable platform-level outcome that survives beyond the campaign window. impact.rationale: The impact case is structurally weak for an SDG brief: '300,000+ Deaf individuals in Japan supported' is the addressable population, not a measured-reach number, and the board provides no usage data — how many Deaf citizens actually received the live January 1, 2024 alert via KIKI, how alert comprehension changed, or any pre/post comprehension study. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the engineering is genuinely first-of-its-kind public-broadcast accessibility infrastructure, but the SDG outcome — actual Deaf citizens reached and comprehension change — is absent from the impact case.

Mechanisms: infrastructure-delivered, purpose-driven-product, partnership-with-multilateral, branded-utility-for-sdg
Idea: A conglomerate involving TBS, a clothing brand that has drawn its inspiration from the sea since 1976, accompanied by the 1 Ocean Foundation, the Paimpolais oceanographic centre and the CNRS national centre for scientific research, all supported by UNESCO. idea.rationale: Re-seeding lifeless seabed rocks by boring holes into them and injecting kelp plants is a tangible, almost agronomic translation of 'reforestation' to underwater ecosystems, and 'turning lifeless rocks into living kelps' is a near-poetic articulation of the mechanism. The idea is fresh as a marine-restoration method but the SDG creative space already has a strong tradition of infrastructure-delivered biodiversity work, so it's distinctive rather than category-redefining.

strategy.rationale: TBS is a French maritime-heritage clothing brand that has drawn its design language from the sea since 1976, so anchoring an ocean-restoration coalition is an idea-mission fit that doesn't read as borrowed purpose, and the work advances SDG 14 (Life Below Water). The brand's strategic move is to convene credible scientific authority — 1 Ocean Foundation, the Paimpolais oceanographic centre, CNRS and UNESCO — rather than speak alone, which is the right play for a small heritage brand making a planetary claim. The context is anchored in cited biodiversity data (700 fish species, 800 algae species, 40% of kelp forests lost since the 1950s), giving the insight a credible data spine.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is operational rather than communicational: drill holes in seabed stones, inject kelp plants, disperse them with a purpose-built vessel, and replicate the method from the Loire-Atlantique coast to Norway and Hawaii. Earned-media amplification reaches a top tier of global outlets (The Guardian, The New York Times, The Washington Post, CNN, Sky News, Euronews, Arte, Brut, Harvard, Geo) which is unusually broad for a regional clothing brand, suggesting the scientific-coalition framing worked as a press unlock.

impact.rationale: 50,000 square metres of kelp forest replanted is an absolute, verifiable surface-area outcome — the correct unit of proof for a reforestation intervention. The '+42M tons of CO2 sequestered each year by healthy kelp forests' figure is a generic ecological capacity claim for kelp at category level rather than a campaign-attributable sequestration outcome, so it should read as the headroom unlocked by the method, not the carbon Rockseeds itself has captured. The international press list is broad and credibly editorial but remains an entrant-reported amplification claim rather than audited reach.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the rock-as-seed mechanism is a tangible biodiversity-infrastructure idea with credible scientific partners, but the headline 42M-tons CO2 figure is kelp-category capacity rather than Rockseeds-attributable sequestration.

idea.rationale: Extending tactile paving from a binary 'sidewalk here' signal into a counted vocabulary of venues (restaurant, bank, pharmacy, bus stop) is a clean, generative reframe of accessibility infrastructure. It treats the sidewalk itself — the brand's actual product — as the medium, and the reframe scales to any city that pours concrete.

strategy.rationale: Sol Cement is Peru's leading cement brand owned by UNACEM, and the campaign sits squarely in SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities) and SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities). Cement-as-accessibility-infrastructure is the rarest kind of strategic fit — the brand has formal authority because it literally manufactures the medium. The board's context (visually impaired pedestrians dependent on others to identify venues) is observationally true but not backed with a specific Peruvian disability-statistics citation.

execution.rationale: The new line-count vocabulary was poured into 75,000 m² of sidewalk in Miraflores, paired with user training for visually impaired pedestrians so the vocabulary is actually decodable. Releasing the pattern system as open-source is the right amplification layer for an infrastructure idea — it shifts the work from a Lima case study to a globally reusable urban standard. The board is light on press distribution beyond design outlets (designboom, Design Indaba), which fits a craft-led infrastructure story rather than a mass-media one.

impact.rationale: The Impact case rests on physical coverage and population served — 75,000 m² implemented, 500,000 people benefited — which are the right absolute units for an accessibility-infrastructure brief. 'Benefited' is a soft denominator (it likely counts all visually impaired residents in Miraflores rather than active users of the new vocabulary), and the board does not disclose adoption rate or any independence-gain measure among trained users. The open-source release is a commitment to future scale rather than a result already delivered.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because a cement brand had the rare authority to redesign the actual sidewalk as the accessibility product, then open-sourced the vocabulary for global civic reuse.

search_doc:
The pregnancy test becomes a testicular-cancer test.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, diagnostic-repurposing, humour-as-health-vehicle, branded-utility-for-sdg, category-jailbreak
Idea: Test-icles is the first campaign that invites men to take a pregnancy test to detect potential testicular cancer. To bring the message to all Mexican men, we created Toxo & Mato, two animated characters inspiring the word 'testicles', a local way of calling testicles. They helped create a fun and entertaining message that launched in national and international media. In addition, influencers received a kit to join the campaign, getting testicular testing a man to get tested.
Insight: A pregnancy test works by detecting the presence of a hormone called beta-subunit of Human Chorionic Gonadotropin, in the urine, which is produced by embryos and some types of testicular cancer. So, if a man tests positive, it means that this hormone is present in his urine, which could indicate potential testicular cancer.

idea.rationale: Repurposing a pharmacy-aisle pregnancy test as a testicular-cancer screening tool — leveraging the genuine biological fact that beta-hCG is produced both by embryos and by some testicular tumours — is a clean diagnostic-repurposing idea that lowers a high-friction health behaviour into a 5-minute drugstore action. The Toxo & Mato cartoon-testicle mascots and the bilingual 'test-icles' wordplay let a stigma-locked health topic travel through humour.

strategy.rationale: Fundación de Alba is a Mexican health charity backed by the Carlos Slim Foundation focused on cancer prevention and detection, so a testicular-cancer screening intervention is direct mission-fit and advances SDG 3 (Good Health and Wellbeing). The insight is the most rigorous on the board — beta-hCG is genuinely the hormone detected by pregnancy tests and is genuinely produced by some testicular tumours, an unusually specific, verifiable biomedical claim — and the context cites Mexico-specific data (3,557 new cases per year, second most deadly cancer for men aged 20-39, 80% don't self-examine). The wordplay 'test-icles' relies on Spanish-language phonetics around the local word for testicles, so cultural-linguistic scope is Mexican / Spanish-speaking rather than globally portable.

execution.rationale: Orchestration leans on the Toxo & Mato animated mascots as a humour vehicle to carry a stigmatised message, an influencer-kit rollout prompting on-camera tests, and a national / international media push concentrated heavily in advertising-trade and Latin-American health-marketing press (Adforum, Adlatina, LatinSpots, Merca2.0, Propmark, PMFarma, RoastBrief). The channel mix skews trade and Spanish-language general media rather than mainstream medical or platform-health partnerships, which limits the patient-funnel infrastructure compared to peer health winners.

impact.rationale: The right business outcome for a cancer-screening intervention is tests actually taken and, downstream, diagnoses caught — neither is documented on the board. The reported outcomes are surface-media metrics: '17.5M+ impressions', '36K+' and '19.8K+' figures whose units are not clearly labelled (engagements? users? conversions?), and the campaign's load-bearing real-world behaviour-change claim (men actually buying and using a pregnancy test for cancer screening, or self-examination rates moving) is absent. The press list is broad within advertising trade but not anchored to medical outlets, so professional clinical validation is also absent.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the pregnancy-test-as-cancer-test idea is a genuine, biologically-grounded diagnostic repurposing, but the impact case is impressions and unlabeled vanity figures rather than tests taken or diagnoses caught.

Idea: An online store, with a patented innovation, can request credit for just five neighbourhood stores by Au & Cards, no guarantees, and with only one customer: trust.
Insight: Mexico's corner shops already operate the country's most resilient credit system — built on personal trust rather than guarantees — and that model can be scaled into a national e-commerce platform.

inferred.qualitative_summary: Scaled Mexico's informal corner-store credit model into a patented national e-commerce platform, channelling support to 72,000 small businesses and reaching 8,500 women / 8,500 families with 15,000 food baskets.

idea.rationale: Scaling the corner-store IOU — Mexico's informal trust-based credit system — into a patented national e-commerce platform is a culturally precise reframe: rather than imposing a foreign banking product on the unbanked, it nationalises the credit mechanic they already use. The idea is original within the Mexican fintech-for-inclusion territory, though it sits in a crowded SDG 1 / SDG 10 lane of micro-credit innovations and the board's articulation of the patent mechanism is slightly fuzzy.

strategy.rationale: WeCapital is a Mexican fintech focused on financial inclusion for the unbanked, with Gahr as the platform-level product; the work targets SDG 1 (No Poverty) and SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities). Mission-fit is direct — a financial-inclusion company building a financial-inclusion product, not borrowing the cause. The insight is anchored by a hard demographic point (more than 88 million unbanked Mexicans) and a culturally specific observation about the neighbourhood-shop credit norm, which is verifiable and locally credible.

execution.rationale: The orchestration centres on the patented online store itself, piloted with five neighbourhood shops as credit nodes and rolled out via Mexican press (Excelsior, El Universal, El Heraldo). The patent and the no-guarantee credit mechanic do the heavy lifting; communications amplification is regional and modest, which is appropriate for an operational fintech product but limits earned scale. The board's description of how the credit underwriting actually works is thin — 'trust' as the single criterion is more brand framing than mechanic disclosure.

impact.rationale: The headline numbers are absolutes rather than percentages — 72,000 small businesses supported, 8,500 women and 8,500 families reached, 15,000 food baskets delivered — which is correct rigour. However the metric units don't add up to a single coherent outcome: 'supported' and 'reached' are softer than 'credit-approved' or 'loan disbursed', and there is no default-rate or volume-of-credit-issued number, which is the obvious business outcome a lender should disclose. For a financial inclusion brief the absence of a peso-volume or repayment metric keeps the case at the activity level rather than the outcome level.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the cultural insight and patented mechanic are credibly inclusion-native, but the impact case substitutes 'reached' for 'lent to' — the financial outcome a credit product should prove is missing.

idea.rationale: Hijacking Google's reCAPTCHA 'Are you human?' interface and recontextualising it as 'Are we human?' over images of Gaza is a sharp platform-jailbreak idea that lands in a single beat. The mechanic is borrowed from a familiar UI pattern, so the originality sits in the recontextualisation rather than the format itself.

strategy.rationale: Amnesty International is a global human-rights NGO with explicit mandate authority on conflict, civilian protection and SDG 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions), so the brand has legitimacy to call for a Gaza ceasefire. The mission-fit is direct — petition-driven pressure is Amnesty's core operating model. The board's framing of October 7th and the subsequent escalation is presented in general terms without specific casualty data points on the board itself, which weakens the insight-veracity layer for an organisation whose strategic asset is rigorous human-rights documentation.

execution.rationale: Execution is a social-media-native series of real conflict images overlaid with the reCAPTCHA interface, sound-designed to immerse viewers, with the petition and a casualty counter on the Amnesty website as the conversion endpoint. The channel choice — social feed mimicking the UX of a routine interaction — is correctly matched to a young digitally-native audience habituated to scrolling past humanitarian content. The campaign is mechanically simple, with a single asset type and a single call-to-action.

impact.rationale: The 3.5 million global signatures and '+123 new monthly donors' are anchored absolutes and the right business outcomes for an NGO petition brief; the 10.5% engagement rate and 216K petition reach are platform-level proxies whose base (engagements on what, reach across which channels) is not disclosed. The +750 daily signatures cannot be benchmarked without a baseline, so it reads as a soft claim. Crucially the actual SDG 16 outcome the petition is for — any movement toward a ceasefire or any documented policy impact — is structurally beyond the campaign's reach, so impact stops at petition collection rather than reaching the cause it advocates.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the reCAPTCHA jailbreak is a sharp interface-recontextualisation and the 3.5M signatures are an anchored petition outcome, but the work cannot demonstrate downstream SDG 16 effect on the conflict it addresses.

inferred.insight: The -18°C frozen-food standard is an arbitrary century-old convention, not a scientific requirement — a global supply chain can decarbonise simply by agreeing to be three degrees warmer.
inferred.one_liner: Move the frozen-food standard from -18 to -15.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Persuaded 60% of the global container-shipping industry to raise the frozen-food temperature standard from -18 to -15°C, unlocking an estimated 17.7M tons of carbon savings and an 11% lift in DP World brand trust.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial layout with a hero Earth globe sandwiched between an icy frozen-pea texture on either side; bold typographic 'The Move to -15°' headline at centre; press-quote band on top; three large metric blocks on white at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Persuading the global cold-chain industry to raise the frozen-food temperature standard by three degrees — from a century-old -18°C convention to -15°C — is a rare 'shift the default' idea that operates at infrastructure rather than consumer level. Naming a precise number ('Move to -15') gives the campaign the structural clarity of a policy ask rather than a brand platform.

strategy.rationale: DP World is the Dubai-based global logistics and ports operator that physically moves much of the world's frozen freight, which makes leading a frozen-supply-chain decarbonisation coalition exact mission-fit and an obvious site of authority, addressing SDG 13 (Climate Action) head-on. The strategic decision to make the academic research freely available and form a neutral, unbranded coalition is the right reading of the underlying insight: in fragmented logistics ecosystems, branded leadership is a barrier — competitors will only join a shared, scientifically credible standard. The insight that -18°C is an arbitrary century-old convention rather than a scientific minimum is the load-bearing claim, and the board grounds it in commissioned independent academic research rather than asserting it.

execution.rationale: The phasing builds the coalition before the noise: commission peer-credible academic research, open-source it, form the neutral unbranded body, then onboard the largest shipping companies, hauliers, warehouses and food producers. Amplification comes through industry press (Cooling Post, Logistic Middle East, ACW, Greenport), business press (Bloomberg, Forbes, Yahoo Finance) and a Denmark PM address at COP — channels matched to the regulators, operators and investors who would actually need to ratify a new norm.

impact.rationale: The headline outcome — 60% of the global container-shipping industry signed up — is the right business proof for a standard-shift campaign, since adoption is the entire mechanism by which carbon savings can materialise. The 17.7M tons CO2 figure is presented as the total carbon saving from the three-degree change, which is a modelled projection rather than a measured outcome and should be read as ceiling potential, not realised reduction. The +11% brand-trust lift for DP World is an entrant-reported brand-tracker metric without a stated base population, so it functions as directional brand benefit rather than hard SDG impact, but the coalition-adoption number alone is durable infrastructure-level change.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold by moving an industry standard rather than an audience: 60% of global container shipping signed up to shift frozen food from -18°C to -15°C — a structural decarbonisation lever, not a campaign.
